items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.

items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.

items.correct: A tall rock pillar in the sea
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A tall rock pillar in the sea. “A type of cave” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Cave + arch + stack — how many landforms in this sequence?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
